Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD sources only two files via HTTPS from trusted, predictable URLs: an AppImage from the GitHub releases page of the project's official repository and an icon from the repository's main branch. Both sources are hosted on github.com under the legitimate organization "CommonWealthRobotics", which aligns with the package's upstream. The `sha256sums` array provides fixed checksums for both files, so no dynamic download integrity bypass occurs. There is no `pkgver()` function, so version resolution is static. The global scope contains no command substitutions or immediate execution constructs (like $(curl ...) or backticks), and no code runs outside the `package()` function, which will not be executed due to `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`. The source fetching and PKGBUILD sourcing during `makepkg --printsrcinfo` is therefore confined to declarative, non-malicious operations.
</details>
<summary>
No dynamic code execution; sources from official GitHub.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No dynamic code execution; sources from official GitHub.[/]

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is straightforward and well-formed. The `source` array fetches an AppImage and a PNG icon directly from the project's own upstream GitHub repository (`github.com/CommonWealthRobotics/CaDoodle`), which matches the declared `url`. Both sources have explicit `sha256sums` pinned (not SKIP), providing integrity verification.

There is no top-level code execution, no command substitution outside of variable interpolation, and no `pkgver()` function. All actual installation logic is confined to `package()`, which will not run during `--nobuild --noprepare`. Nothing in this file performs network exfiltration, obfuscated execution, or unexpected system modifications.
</details>
<summary>Standard AppImage packaging with pinned checksums from upstream; fully safe.</summary>
</security_assessment>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard binary package using an AppImage from the project's official GitHub releases. The `source` array downloads the AppImage and an icon file over HTTPS directly from `github.com/CommonWealthRobotics/CaDoodle`, which is consistent with the project's upstream. The `sha256sums` are provided and match the fixed release tag `${pkgver}`, ensuring integrity. No command substitution, code execution, or network operations occur in the top-level scope or in a `pkgver()` function (which is absent and thus not executed). The only executed code during sourcing is declarative metadata assignment, which is safe. Fetching the sources via `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` would not trigger any malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>
No execution during source fetch; all is declarative and verifiable.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No execution during source fetch; all is declarative and verifiable.[/]

data.reasoning: Both models confirmed PKGBUILD safety with static sources from trusted GitHub URLs and checksums. Failure to download sources (likely transient) doesn't indicate malice. No dangerous code paths in reviewed PKGBUILD.
coverage_issues: Failed to review AppImage/icon files due to download failure - critical gap as these execute during installation
false_negatives: Potential risks in unreviewed AppImage (could contain malicious payload) and install-time behavior
learnings: Improve source download reliability for binary analysis
Prioritize executable binaries over metadata files when present
Clarify in reports when critical files remain unanalyzed due to technical failures
